serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pengat is approximately 200 g (one bowl), containing roughly 364 kcal, 23.7 g carbohydrates, and 29.0 g fat from coconut milk.

nutrient_source.title: Pengat — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pengat (pumpkin and sweet potato in coconut milk and sugar). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet potato and coconut milk-based desserts — analogous estimate based on sweet potato GI (GI ~54) and coconut milk modulation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pengat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the primary carbohydrate contributors: sweet potato (GI ~54, University of Sydney GI Database) and pumpkin (GI ~64–75 for boiled pumpkin). The high fat content from coconut milk (~14.5 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and moderates the overall glycaemic response, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (55 × 11.83 g carbohydrate × 2) / 100 = 13.0 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~12.5 g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium is low at 58 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pengat to small portions due to its high saturated fat content from coconut milk, which can contribute to arterial stiffness and endothelial dysfunction; request a reduced-coconut-milk version and limit added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 7.45 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.18 g/100g) and low GI (~55) from sweet potato and pumpkin base, creating a meaningful glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Pengat only occasionally and in small portions (100 g or less), as the combination of added sugar, low-GI starchy vegetables, and low fibre produces a significant glycaemic load; substituting palm sugar with a low-GI sweetener and reducing portion size can help manage postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 12.5 g/100g from coconut milk, exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold associated with elevated LDL-cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Pengat because its saturated fat content (~12.5 g/100g) from coconut milk significantly raises LDL-cholesterol; if consumed, a version made with light coconut milk and reduced sugar is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pengat is a traditional Malay and Singaporean sweet dessert soup made by simmering starchy root vegetables and fruits — most commonly pumpkin (labu) and sweet potato (ubi keledek) — in rich coconut milk sweetened with sugar or palm sugar (gula melaka), often perfumed with pandan leaves. The dish has a creamy, mildly sweet broth with soft, yielding pieces of vegetable that absorb the coconut milk during cooking. Texturally it is smooth and comforting, with the natural sweetness of pumpkin and sweet potato balanced by the richness of coconut milk. Pengat is a popular Malay kuih and dessert served at Hari Raya celebrations, community gatherings, and as an everyday snack or after-meal sweet. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 182 kcal, 14.49 g fat (predominantly saturated fat ~12.5 g from coconut milk), 11.83 g carbohydrates, 7.45 g sugar, 1.3 g protein, and 1.18 g dietary fibre. Its high saturated fat content makes it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis, while its low glycaemic index (~55) and added sugar make it a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is low at 58 mg/100g. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact match to Pengat (pumpkin and sweet potato in coconut milk and sugar). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using sweet potato and pumpkin GI values, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Pengat are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at ~12.5 g/100g based on coconut milk composition (approximately 86% of coconut milk fat is saturated) applied to the reported total fat of 14.49 g/100g; this estimate is consistent with the input record's health suitability notes. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for Pengat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 11.83 × 2) / 100 = 13.0 g. The input record referenced sago pearls and banana as possible ingredients; the HPB source entry describes pumpkin and sweet potato only — the canonical description follows the HPB source. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code for Pengat exists.
